metadata.summary: Four-stage framework (Introduction → Growth → Maturity → Decline) for diagnosing product lifecycle stage and applying the matching investment strategy.

content.title: Product Lifecycle Management
content.summary: A four-stage framework (Introduction → Growth → Maturity → Decline) for diagnosing which lifecycle stage a product is in and applying the matching investment strategy. Each stage has distinct focus areas, key metrics, and transition triggers. The process runs in four steps: assess stage from metrics, validate with multi-quarter trends, apply stage-appropriate strategy, and plan the next-stage transition.

why.p: Products managed with the same tactics regardless of maturity stage waste investment. Growth acquisition tactics applied to a Maturity product over-spend on CAC; optimization investments applied to an Introduction product starve PMF discovery. Stage-aware allocation is the lever: right strategy, right metrics, right expectations per stage.

list.item: Quarterly portfolio review — decide investment level for each shipped product and stop applying growth budget to maturity or decline assets.
A product hits a metrics inflection (growth slows from 30% to 8%, churn jumps) — need a structured stage reassessment, not a gut call.
Pre-roadmap step: tag every product with its stage before sequencing the year.
End-of-life decision: product declining for 3+ quarters, need a sunset plan with migration timeline.
Investor or board update: defending why a Maturity product gets retention budget instead of new features.
Stage transition checkpoint: Introduction product hits PMF and must shift from "learn fast" to "scale" tooling.
Pre-PMF startup with one product still hunting for fit — there is no lifecycle yet, only a discovery loop.
Sub-feature decisions inside one product (which feature to build next) — RICE / MoSCoW are sharper at that grain.
Pure B2B services / agency revenue with no productized asset — lifecycle math is undefined.
Internal tools / platforms with no external customers — they have a usefulness curve, not a revenue curve.
Crisis weeks (P0 outage, security incident) — survival first, restore lifecycle planning after.

section.name: introduction-stage
section.p: Low revenue, negative profit, early adopters only, high uncertainty, frequent pivots.

rule.statement: In Introduction, maximize learning and product-market fit validation over revenue extraction. Invest 70%+ in iteration and PMF, 0% in sunset activities.
rule.rationale: Pre-PMF, every dollar on acquisition before fit is wasted CAC. The stage ends only when activation and early retention signal demand.

list.item: Key metrics: activation rate, early retention (Day 7), NPS from early users.
Stage signal: MRR < $10k OR growth > 50% YoY AND profitability negative AND <12 months since launch.

section.name: growth-stage
section.p: Rapidly increasing revenue, market acceptance, competition increasing, scaling challenges.

rule.statement: In Growth, invest 60%+ in customer acquisition and feature expansion to capture market share before competition intensifies. Build team and infrastructure in parallel.
rule.rationale: Growth windows close fast; under-investing in acquisition during Growth produces a smaller Maturity base from which to defend.

list.item: Key metrics: growth rate, CAC, LTV, market share, revenue.
Stage signal: growth >= 20% YoY for 2+ quarters AND profitability improving AND competition increasing.

section.name: maturity-stage
section.p: Stable or slowing revenue, market saturation, intense competition, optimization focus.

rule.statement: In Maturity, shift 50%+ of investment to retention and optimization; cap new acquisition at <30%. Measure profitability and efficiency ratios, not growth rate.
rule.rationale: New customer acquisition becomes expensive in saturated markets; retaining existing customers and extracting margin is the highest-ROI lever.

list.item: Key metrics: profitability, churn rate, NPS, cost efficiency ratios.
Stage signal: growth < 15% YoY AND profitability at peak AND saturated market signals.

section.name: decline-stage
section.p: Decreasing revenue, technology shift, customer migration, resource reallocation.

rule.statement: In Decline, invest 60%+ in optimization and sunset activities; stop new acquisition investment. Focus on cash harvesting, migration support, and sunset timeline.
rule.rationale: Continued acquisition spend in Decline produces negative-ROI customers who still need support through the sunset window.

list.item: Key metrics: cash flow, migration rate, support cost, timeline to sunset.
Stage signal: growth < 0 for 2+ quarters AND customer migration evidence.

rule.statement: Anchor every stage label to at least two evidence sources (e.g., MRR series + cohort retention). Single-metric staging is the #1 cause of misclassification.
rule.rationale: Single metrics are gamed or misread; two independent sources provide triangulation.

rule.statement: Re-classify quarterly. SaaS stage transitions can happen in two quarters; annual reviews apply the wrong strategy for 9-12 months.
rule.rationale: Stage transitions are not annual events; quarterly cadence matches the speed at which market conditions shift.

antipattern.description: Applying growth acquisition tactics to a Decline-stage product.
antipattern.reason: Acquisition spend compounds losses — acquired customers still generate support cost during the sunset window with no long-term LTV payback.

antipattern.description: Refusing to declare Decline because the label is politically costly — lobbying for "late Maturity" indefinitely.
antipattern.reason: Delayed Decline classification extends over-investment; pre-commit numeric kill thresholds (e.g., "MRR YoY < 0 for 3 quarters = automatic Decline review") to remove the political judgment call.

rule.statement: Collect at least 4-8 quarters of MRR, growth rate, churn, gross margin, and market share data before classifying. With fewer than 4 quarters of history, default to Intro with low confidence.
rule.rationale: Stage transitions can happen in 2 quarters; short histories produce high-variance classifications that reverse on the next data point.

list.item: Intro signal: MRR < $10k OR growth > 50% YoY AND negative profitability AND <12mo since launch.
Growth signal: growth >= 20% YoY for 2+ quarters AND improving profitability AND increasing competition.
Maturity signal: growth < 15% YoY AND peak profitability AND saturated market signals.
Decline signal: growth < 0 for 2+ quarters AND customer migration evidence.

rule.statement: Review metrics trends over 3+ quarters, compare to market benchmarks, consider external factors (category shift, new technology), and get cross-functional team alignment before finalizing the stage label.
rule.rationale: Single-person classification is subject to optimism bias; team alignment surfaces conflicting signals that a solo reviewer misses.

antipattern.description: Using revenue YoY growth as the sole classification signal for network-effect or bundling products.
antipattern.reason: Network-effect products can show low revenue but high engagement (still Growth); bundling products can show stable revenue while the real value is migrating to a different SKU.

rule.statement: Explicitly map current investments (% engineering cost on acquisition / retention / optimization / sunset) against the stage-appropriate allocation rubric. Flag and resolve any misalignment before next quarter planning.
rule.rationale: Investment misalignment — growth tactics in Decline, optimization tactics in Intro — is the primary source of wasted product budget at the portfolio level.

rule.statement: Pre-commit numeric transition triggers when entering Maturity, not when entering Decline: e.g., "if MRR YoY < 0 for 3 consecutive quarters, automatic Decline review." Document in portfolio decisions log.
rule.rationale: By the time Decline is obvious, the org has already over-invested for 2-4 quarters. Pre-committed thresholds remove the political delay.

rule.statement: Treat stage transitions as bidirectional. A Maturity product can re-enter Growth via a new segment, pricing tier, or 10x feature unlock. Always evaluate revival vs. sunset before committing to harvest mode.
rule.rationale: Default-to-sunset is expensive when the underlying user need still exists. Re-platforming can recover Maturity-equivalent performance for 20% of new-product cost.

example.title: SaaS Product in Growth
example.note: Assessment: 40% YoY growth, just reached profitability, 3 new competitors entered the market in the last two quarters.
Stage: Growth (confirmed by 2 independent signals: YoY growth > 20% + improving profitability).
Strategy: Accelerate customer acquisition, expand feature set for enterprise segment, build sales team, invest in infrastructure to support scale.
Transition trigger: Growth slows to < 15% YoY for two consecutive quarters — begin Maturity strategy shift (reduce acquisition %, increase retention %).

example.title: Legacy Product in Decline
example.note: Assessment: -10% YoY revenue, customers actively migrating to a newer platform, core technology becoming outdated.
Stage: Decline (confirmed by 2 independent signals: negative YoY growth + customer migration evidence).
Strategy: Maintain core functionality only, help customers migrate, reduce support costs, plan explicit sunset timeline of 18 months.
End goal: Complete migration of all active users within 18 months; communicate sunset date 6 months in advance; wind down support contracts at sunset.

antipattern.description: Same metrics tracked regardless of lifecycle stage.
antipattern.reason: Growth rate is the primary metric in Introduction and Growth but meaningless in Maturity (where profitability and churn matter more) and actively misleading in Decline.

antipattern.description: Emotional attachment to a declining product preventing objective sunset decision.
antipattern.reason: Emotional attachment delays the inevitable, extends over-investment, and wastes capacity that could accelerate a Growth-stage product. Pre-committed numeric thresholds are the antidote.

antipattern.description: No sunset plan drafted until Decline is obvious.
antipattern.reason: Sunset planning requires 6-12 months of lead time for customer communication, contract wind-down, and team transition. Waiting until Decline is confirmed leaves insufficient runway.

template.purpose: Metrics table, stage indicator matrix, assessed stage block, transition plan
Focus areas, key metrics, investment priorities, risks, success criteria per stage

script.purpose: Classifies products by lifecycle stage from quarterly MRR CSV; emits ambiguous rows for LLM review
